speaker: interviewer
text: Six survivors. Abraham Bomba. The date is August 14th 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something I forgot to mention is about a man by the name of [? Capita ?] [? Mugello. ?] He was almost the head man from the Czechoslovak army. He came over there. According my knowledge, he [? met ?] his wife, because his wife was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Treblinka. He was working there. He was in the same barrack with me. He was one of the head men to organize the uprising in Treblinka, which actually, the uprising was on August 2nd 1943, which I wasn't there already. I escaped in January of 1943. But after the war I find out that he was one of the leader. It is my responsibility to mention his name at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- but you did know him when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I know him. I spoke to him many times at night, when we were together in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you speak about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the situation. The way it is. What to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the time that he was there and you were there, was he already beginning to think about leading the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, he was together-- he was together with [? Horajinski. ?] He was together with [? Gilleski. ?] He was together with [? Mitrakovsky. ?] He was also together with the [? Masarik, ?] which they were very close friend, and a few more of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- now, to move forward again, we're talking about-- you were now back in Chezstochowa and you were now involved in organizing an uprising in the small ghetto of Czestochowa. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: When it happened, we had an organization, an underground organization, in Czestochowa. There were some leaders of them. One of them, I would like to mention a woman by the name of Rifka Glanz. There was a few men. A very close friend of mine, we were together in the concentration camp-- I mean, before the war even. He was a leader of the Zionist organization, by the name of Nathan Schlamnitsky, with his brother. There was from various organization, like in the ghetto was organized one party which has to organize in organizing the uprising, whatever we could. And that was the organization from one communist Zionist religious organization. We had one organization that is the Jewish Underground, by that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any weapons? Were you able to obtain any weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some weapons. But the story with weapons is very tragic, because for no money we could get it. And this we could buy, only by the Pollocks. And the Pollocks. Were not interested of-- of our survival. We had some.

speaker: interviewer
text: What weapons did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just guns. And then we started to make our own Molotov bottles, if you know what that is.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Molotov-- the Molotov you fill in with-- how you call? Oil. Or you fill in with some kind of gas. And by throwing them, they explode. Well, it happened also that by a selection the one which went from Czestochowa, one of our men from Treblinka by the name of Fislewicz, had a gun. And when they selected people, a number of people, say a few hundred of it, he got out from his group. Like there were groups, 25, 30 in a group. And he opened fire on a German. But the gun was so good they couldn't kill them. At that time, they took out 27 from the boys, from our boys. The finest. And in front of us, they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, we went in back to the ghetto where we worked about another couple months. And we had a man which was really one of the finest personal friend of mine. One of them was Gamolinski, and the other one I don't recall the name. How can I forget the name like that? Will to come to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They caught him, and they find out he is the organizer. They ripped him apart. But they couldn't get out a word of him. It was a day before that, on that 26 of June, 1943, they liquidated the small ghetto. They bombed all the houses in the small ghetto, which was left, and a lot of people perished at the bunkers.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding. With some kids, too. And the ghetto did not exist anymore. A number of the people were killed. And the big majority went in to the concentration camp, the labor camp, by the name of HASAG.

speaker: interviewer
text: HASAG?

speaker: interviewee
text: HASAG.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Czestochowa. HASAG is Hugo Schneider Aktiengesellschaft. That's where they first let us come from, that one of the big factories from ammunition in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, I went over there too. And we worked. Condition was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it? What-- what did this camp-- was it a labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a labor camp. Mostly--

speaker: interviewer
text: For men and men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Men and women, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly people worked ammunition, [? they ?] [? worked ?] [? out. ?] I myself, I didn't work in the factory. I worked in the camp as a barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cutting whose hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cutting-- cutting hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of who? Prisoners or of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Men. Men. Prisoners only. Nobody else could come in over there. So the-- who guarded the camp? The German [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And they never asked you to cut their hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. There was some barbers. I never cut the German hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some barbers--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was this camp surrounded by barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the barracks like that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks. We were in the one barrack, about a couple hundred people. Laying down, it was like one lay, second, then a third one. Each body to-- body to body.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you now still with all your friends, most of your friends that had planned the uprising? Were you all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: --together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was most of the friends, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job that you were assigned to in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the barber

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I just a barber. I had no assignment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fed in this camp? Food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly, but to go by. To go by with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the kommandant of this camp? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It will-- kommandant was one German by the name of Dr. Litt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Litt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Litt. But if he would be alive, I myself, and hundred of people more will testify not against him, but for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was a nice, kind man. Where we starved to get bread, he got the bread for us for a very small price. And he did a lot for us. There's no one from that camp which would say a bad word against him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did he do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing else. He couldn't do nothing more, because on top of him, he had the gestapo. But generally, the camp in Czestochowa considered to be one of the best camp in existence of the German concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything else happen to you while you were in this camp, HASAG?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing specially. Nothing. I was-- I was assigned to work in a hospital by people who had typhus. And I worked with them. And beside this, I worked also in the barbershop. Established a barber shop exactly like a barber shop from outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave you tools?

speaker: interviewee
text: And worked there. Yes, old tools. [INAUDIBLE] We had always. Nobody took away from us. And we did that-- that job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any punishments at all in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't say I did. I know that people got killed, shot. I know they almost killed a woman also, she got shot in their eye. But I wouldn't say that I was to see it, because they were outside in the factory, and I was in the place assigned to-- for the barbershop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to escape from this camp that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing that I know of. I tell you why. There were no place where to escape. If you say escape, you got to go somewhere. To be-- to be outside between the Pollocks was even worse than to be by the Germans. So in other word, the escaping didn't mean a thing. To escape, you got to have a place where to be hidden. If you have no place like that, forget about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have-- at this point in HASAG, did you have hopes that you were going to survive at this point? What were your feelings--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. In my mind, I always had. Even in the toughest time, I had. I had always in my mind that I'm going to survive. I don't know why, but that was. And we-- we were there I would say a long time until January 16, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? But ?] we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: By who?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russian. But before the liberation, I escaped from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was the night before the liberation. And they supposed to get rid of all the people there by exploding the factory with everything. And myself and more people saw they're carrying the bombs for it. But they had no time to do it. The Russians were on top of them. Around 10 o'clock at night, I went, at that time. I went with a girl, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I met her in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met--

speaker: interviewee
text: The small ghetto. When I got back, she came over. And she asked me about her husband. She was married before that. It happened that I find out that her husband was some kind of cousin of mine. And he was at Treblinka. He worked. And I told her the story, how it is. After he worked for a while over there, he got killed. And we got, you know, together. Like coming. And it wasn't so-- so simple, even in the ghetto. Which I'm far off the ghetto. There-- in the ghetto were three streets. One street was for men. The second street was for married couple. And the third street were for ladies, unmarried ladies. In another word is if somebody-- like it happened to a relative of mine who lived in the street where the men lived. He wanted to see his sister which is on the street of the girls. They killed him, because it was not allowed. There was only three streets over there. It happened she was working-- I was in a barbershop also in the-- in the ghetto. In the small ghetto I worked in a barbershop. She should come-- she could come in, doing a cleaning or other things. So we met and we got together. It's already over 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- were you married in the ghetto, the small ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who married you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a rabbi over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Regina Hamburger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you--

speaker: interviewee
text: She is from Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was right after I got back from traveling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were both in, then, HASAG together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were working. We work in HASAG. We could see each other in the HASAG, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you escaped together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, when I planned this, because I didn't want-- I told her. Listen to me. I will never go into a [INAUDIBLE] again. Even if I get killed, I don't go in. If I escape, OK. If not, let them kill me. But don't go with-- don't go after me, because there is no such thing like they're taking men and woman together. Well, it happened that before I left the barbed wire, and there was a big stop already between the Ukrainian and the Germans. I said, hey, come. We go together. And the following morning, we were liberated by the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like when you-- how did you know you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we know is [? were ?] the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they said they're going-- they're going to Berlin to liberate [INAUDIBLE]. There was, you know, some kind of joy. But some kind of thing that-- first thing, we were left alone without nobody, without family, without clothes, without money, without anything. And the first few days, it was very hard even with food. We had nothing to eat. In the concentration camp, at least we had a piece of bread, a soup, little water, something. But outside, we didn't have nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians didn't give you any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have themselves. They slept in-- in-- in February-- January, February. They slept in street. And they didn't go in to take some apartments or tell people to go out [? of them. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go? After liberation, where did and your wife go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the liberation, I was my home town. But I couldn't stand it. Every place, every little street I went by, it was some kind of mix up in my mind, because I saw people [? was ?] who were relatives, which in my mind. Which actually, they were dead. I left the place. I got in touch with a few more barbers, and went back to that place I come from, Beuthen. And we took over a barbershop and a beauty shop from a German woman, which she was willing, very, very willing to give it to us, and not to give to Poles or to give to Russian. And she worked over there with us. And that happened from February until up to June. And then, in Poland, it was created an army. Army [NON-ENGLISH] army, they call themselves. And what they did is to kill Jewish people after the liberation, which a lot of Jewish people got killed. Over 3,000 in Poland. 3,2000 Jewish people got killed after the liberation. It happened across the street where I-- I had the shop. Two of them, officers, came in to a jewelry store without demanding anything, without taking anything. They just took out their guns and they killed them. I said, that time, to my wife, we have to leave. Same night, makes ready, and went to the German border. We maked our papers over there and went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Germany, we went to a place called before Baumberg. It was-- is in Germany. And from there we went to a little place by the name of Falkenstein, which is near Innsbruck. Famous city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you in a displaced persons camp at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, just at the end, before going to United State. I was a few months in a place by the name of Foehrenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Foehrenwald for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say about four or five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at all in this DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like inside this DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: According to camp, it wasn't too bad. We had the UNRRA, which you know, they supplied us with food, packages, clothing, and for the babies. And we had-- there was also some kind of organization, the-- some Zionist organization which had tried to bring people to-- illegal to Israel. It was just like life in a small Jewish shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children at all yet, there? In-- in Foehrenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just the two of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to America '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come? On what ship? Or did you come by plane? What ship--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, came by ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ship General Eltinge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you dock? Where did the ship come to? What harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: To New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where- where did you settle in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we got-- we had-- my wife had-- had a brother, [? but ?] he himself wasn't settled yet. So we went into [? a house. ?] So my wife stayed in a very big room with another 600, 700 women. And I was also at that kind of-- amount of men together. Until we find ourselves something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children? Did you eventually have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bonnie. Bonnie [? Tobie ?] after my mother. She is in Florida now. She has two daughters and a small child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what occupation did you take up in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to America, did you tell people in America-- the American people-- the experience you had been though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I tried, but I-- I couldn't do it. Because first thing, they didn't believe me. The second thing, they said that there's something wrong with-- not only with-- not with me, with some other people the same thing. And that what we were in a concentration camp there was a reason that the German took us to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your adjustment like in America in the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard for us. It was very hard to get an apartment. It was very hard to get a job also. I started working $15 a week. But we managed. I thank-- thank god we didn't need nothing from nobody. We didn't get anything from nobody. We weren't-- we wasn't supported, not by a jury, not by the Jewish Federation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- or were you involved years later, in any sort of trials or anything of any of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --leaders in Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was called to be a witness of the one that was the first one-- it was, if I'm not mistaken, 1951 of a trial from a man from the Treblinka extermination camp by the name of Josef Hirtreiter. That trial was in Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you testify there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I testified to that time, first thing, in Germany was a little simple like it is here in the United States. In the United States, they bring the men in front. He sits next to the judge or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over there, no. Over there, when I came in, we were about five, six witnesses. Over-- I just-- I just say what I-- when it-- what happened to me. When I came into trial, the judge ask your name. So and so. You are a witness for this and this? Yes. Will you recognize the man? I said, I don't know. It's quite a few years. Maybe. No, he said, do you see the man here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man sit in middle of the public. There's 500, 600 people. He wears civil clothes like anybody else. But the eyes you can never forget. And I pointed out at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you recognized him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [? they ?] callled him in on the bench. And at that time I-- I testified. Another time I was over this in Dusseldorf against 10 or 11 from Treblinka. One of them in court, [? Franz ?] [NON-ENGLISH] was [NON-ENGLISH], was [NON-ENGLISH], was-- I don't remember all the name of that. At that time, the same thing we testified. And they were find guilty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- some of them, they got life. Three of them got life. The other got 15 years, 10 years, 6 years. One got free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who got free?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know the name. But I-- I was there when he came out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that feel, seeing those people on trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seeing the people on trial, that is-- that's a difficult. You see the men which went around all day long with a gun, killing people. And he says he is not guilty, he didn't do anything. He just did what the Fuhrer told him to do. He had an order. Just according to order, they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Kommandant Stangl that you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just saw him outside going around. But I never had any word with him. I never--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he at the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: --spoke him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he one of the defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, no. He wasn't at the trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was not--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never-- I never know what had happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were the-- that was the only trial-- two trials that you participated in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the-- just the two trials attended, yes. That's more than enough for me. Believe me, when you get back from a trial like that, you are more sick than alive. It's not a pleasure. Even coming here, believe me, it's not a pleasure. Inside, even if you say with a smile all this, that, inside you can never forget. You will never forget what a turmoil you went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to end this tape and continue.
